You create a new report for your large team in Google Data Studio 360. The report uses Google BigQuery as its data source. It is company policy to ensure employees can view only the data associated with their region, so you create and populate a table for each region. You need to enforce the regional access policy to the data.
Which two actions should you take? (Choose two.)
choice_A: Ensure all the tables are included in global dataset.
choice_B: Ensure each table is included in a dataset for a region.
choice_C: Adjust the settings for each table to allow a related region-based security group view access.
choice_D: Adjust the settings for each view to allow a related region-based security group view access.
choice_E: Adjust the settings for each dataset to allow a related region-based security group view access.
explanation: The correct answers are B and E because they align with the need to enforce regional access policies effectively. By ensuring each table is included in a dataset for a region (Choice B), you can manage data access at the dataset level, which is more efficient and scalable than managing individual table permissions. Adjusting the settings for each dataset to allow a related region-based security group view access (Choice E) ensures that only users from the appropriate region-based security group can view the data, adhering to the company's policy of regional data access. This approach leverages Google Cloud's IAM capabilities to manage access control efficiently at the dataset level.

question_text:
MJTelco Case Study -

Company Overview -
MJTelco is a startup that plans to build networks in rapidly growing, underserved markets around the world. The company has patents for innovative optical communications hardware. Based on these patents, they can create many reliable, high-speed backbone links with inexpensive hardware.

Company Background -
Founded by experienced telecom executives, MJTelco uses technologies originally developed to overcome communications challenges in space. Fundamental to their operation, they need to create a distributed data infrastructure that drives real-time analysis and incorporates machine learning to continuously optimize their topologies. Because their hardware is inexpensive, they plan to overdeploy the network allowing them to account for the impact of dynamic regional politics on location availability and cost.
Their management and operations teams are situated all around the globe creating many-to-many relationship between data consumers and provides in their system. After careful consideration, they decided public cloud is the perfect environment to support their needs.

Solution Concept -
MJTelco is running a successful proof-of-concept (PoC) project in its labs. They have two primary needs:
✑ Scale and harden their PoC to support significantly more data flows generated when they ramp to more than 50,000 installations.
✑ Refine their machine-learning cycles to verify and improve the dynamic models they use to control topology definition.
MJTelco will also use three separate operating environments `" development/test, staging, and production `" to meet the needs of running experiments, deploying new features, and serving production customers.

Business Requirements -
✑ Scale up their production environment with minimal cost, instantiating resources when and where needed in an unpredictable, distributed telecom user community.
✑ Ensure security of their proprietary data to protect their leading-edge machine learning and analysis.
✑ Provide reliable and timely access to data for analysis from distributed research workers
✑ Maintain isolated environments that support rapid iteration of their machine-learning models without affecting their customers.

Technical Requirements -
Ensure secure and efficient transport and storage of telemetry data
//IMG//

✑ Rapidly scale instances to support between 10,000 and 100,000 data providers with multiple flows each.
✑ Allow analysis and presentation against data tables tracking up to 2 years of data storing approximately 100m records/day
✑ Support rapid iteration of monitoring infrastructure focused on awareness of data pipeline problems both in telemetry flows and in production learning cycles.

CEO Statement -
Our business model relies on our patents, analytics and dynamic machine learning. Our inexpensive hardware is organized to be highly reliable, which gives us cost advantages. We need to quickly stabilize our large distributed data pipelines to meet our reliability and capacity commitments.

CTO Statement -
Our public cloud services must operate as advertised. We need resources that scale and keep our data secure. We also need environments in which our data scientists can carefully study and quickly adapt our models. Because we rely on automation to process our data, we also need our development and test environments to work as we iterate.

CFO Statement -
The project is too large for us to maintain the hardware and software required for the data and analysis. Also, we cannot afford to staff an operations team to monitor so many data feeds, so we will rely on automation and infrastructure. Google Cloud's machine learning will allow our quantitative researchers to work on our high-value problems instead of problems with our data pipelines.
MJTelco needs you to create a schema in Google Bigtable that will allow for the historical analysis of the last 2 years of records. Each record that comes in is sent every 15 minutes, and contains a unique identifier of the device and a data record. The most common query is for all the data for a given device for a given day.
Which schema should you use?
choice_A: Rowkey: date#device_id Column data: data_point
choice_B: Rowkey: date Column data: device_id, data_point
choice_C: Rowkey: device_id Column data: date, data_point
choice_D: Rowkey: data_point Column data: device_id, date
choice_E: Rowkey: date#data_point Column data: device_id
explanation: The correct schema choice is A: Rowkey: date#device_id Column data: data_point. This design optimizes for the most common query, which is retrieving all data for a given device on a specific day. By concatenating the date and device_id in the row key, the schema allows efficient retrieval of all records for a device on a particular day, leveraging Bigtable's row key-based lookup capabilities. This approach ensures that data is stored in a way that aligns with the query pattern, minimizing the need for scanning large amounts of data and improving query performance. Other options, such as B, C, D, and E, do not align the row key with the query pattern as effectively, leading to less efficient data retrieval.

question_text: Your company has recently grown rapidly and now ingesting data at a significantly higher rate than it was previously. You manage the daily batch MapReduce analytics jobs in Apache Hadoop. However, the recent increase in data has meant the batch jobs are falling behind. You were asked to recommend ways the development team could increase the responsiveness of the analytics without increasing costs. What should you recommend they do?
choice_A: Rewrite the job in Pig.
choice_B: Rewrite the job in Apache Spark.
choice_C: Increase the size of the Hadoop cluster.
choice_D: Decrease the size of the Hadoop cluster but also rewrite the job in Hive.
explanation: Rewriting the job in Apache Spark (Choice B) is the correct recommendation because Spark is designed for faster data processing compared to traditional MapReduce. Spark processes data in-memory, which significantly reduces the time taken for iterative and interactive analytics tasks, making it more responsive for large-scale data processing. Unlike Pig (Choice A) and Hive (Choice D), which still rely on MapReduce under the hood, Spark provides a more efficient execution model. Increasing the size of the Hadoop cluster (Choice C) would increase costs, which contradicts the requirement to avoid additional expenses. Therefore, leveraging Spark's capabilities can enhance performance without the need for additional hardware investment.

question_text: You work for a large fast food restaurant chain with over 400,000 employees. You store employee information in Google BigQuery in a Users table consisting of a FirstName field and a LastName field. A member of IT is building an application and asks you to modify the schema and data in BigQuery so the application can query a FullName field consisting of the value of the FirstName field concatenated with a space, followed by the value of the LastName field for each employee. How can you make that data available while minimizing cost?
choice_A: Create a view in BigQuery that concatenates the FirstName and LastName field values to produce the FullName.
choice_B: Add a new column called FullName to the Users table. Run an UPDATE statement that updates the FullName column for each user with the concatenation of the FirstName and LastName values.
choice_C: Create a Google Cloud Dataflow job that queries BigQuery for the entire Users table, concatenates the FirstName value and LastName value for each user, and loads the proper values for FirstName, LastName, and FullName into a new table in BigQuery.
choice_D: Use BigQuery to export the data for the table to a CSV file. Create a Google Cloud Dataproc job to process the CSV file and output a new CSV file containing the proper values for FirstName, LastName and FullName. Run a BigQuery load job to load the new CSV file into BigQuery.
explanation: The correct answer is A. Creating a view in BigQuery that concatenates the FirstName and LastName field values to produce the FullName is the most cost-effective and efficient solution. A view is a virtual table that does not store data itself but provides a way to present data from existing tables in a specific format, minimizing storage costs and avoiding data duplication. This approach allows the application to query the FullName field without altering the existing table schema or incurring additional costs associated with data processing or storage, as would be the case with options B, C, and D. Options B, C, and D involve modifying the table schema, running update operations, or creating new tables, all of which can increase costs and complexity.

question_text:
You are deploying a new storage system for your mobile application, which is a media streaming service. You decide the best fit is Google Cloud Datastore. You have entities with multiple properties, some of which can take on multiple values. For example, in the entity 'Movie' the property 'actors' and the property
'tags' have multiple values but the property 'date released' does not. A typical query would ask for all movies with actor=<actorname> ordered by date_released or all movies with tag=Comedy ordered by date_released. How should you avoid a combinatorial explosion in the number of indexes?
choice_A: Manually configure the index in your index config as follows:
choice_B: Manually configure the index in your index config as follows:
choice_C: Set the following in your entity options: exclude_from_indexes = 'actors, tags'
choice_D: Set the following in your entity options: exclude_from_indexes = 'date_published'
explanation: The correct answer is A because manually configuring the index allows you to specify composite indexes that are necessary for your queries without creating unnecessary indexes that could lead to a combinatorial explosion. In Google Cloud Datastore, when you have properties with multiple values, such as 'actors' and 'tags', creating automatic indexes for every possible combination can result in a large number of indexes, which is inefficient. By manually configuring the index, you can specifically create indexes for the queries you need, such as those filtering by 'actor' or 'tag' and ordering by 'date_released', thus optimizing performance and storage. Choices C and D, which involve excluding properties from indexes, would prevent you from querying those properties efficiently, which is not suitable for your use case.

question_text:
You work for a manufacturing plant that batches application log files together into a single log file once a day at 2:00 AM. You have written a Google Cloud
Dataflow job to process that log file. You need to make sure the log file in processed once per day as inexpensively as possible. What should you do?
choice_A: Change the processing job to use Google Cloud Dataproc instead.
choice_B: Manually start the Cloud Dataflow job each morning when you get into the office.
choice_C: Create a cron job with Google App Engine Cron Service to run the Cloud Dataflow job.
choice_D: Configure the Cloud Dataflow job as a streaming job so that it processes the log data immediately.
explanation: The correct answer is C. Creating a cron job with Google App Engine Cron Service to run the Cloud Dataflow job ensures that the log file is processed automatically once per day at a specified time, such as shortly after the log file is batched at 2:00 AM. This approach is cost-effective because it automates the process without requiring manual intervention, unlike option B, which involves manually starting the job each morning. Option A, using Google Cloud Dataproc, is not necessary as Cloud Dataflow is already suitable for batch processing and is likely more cost-effective for this specific task. Option D, configuring the job as a streaming job, would be more expensive and unnecessary since the logs are batched daily, not continuously generated.

question_text:
You work for an economic consulting firm that helps companies identify economic trends as they happen. As part of your analysis, you use Google BigQuery to correlate customer data with the average prices of the 100 most common goods sold, including bread, gasoline, milk, and others. The average prices of these goods are updated every 30 minutes. You want to make sure this data stays up to date so you can combine it with other data in BigQuery as cheaply as possible.
What should you do?
choice_A: Load the data every 30 minutes into a new partitioned table in BigQuery.
choice_B: Store and update the data in a regional Google Cloud Storage bucket and create a federated data source in BigQuery
choice_C: Store the data in Google Cloud Datastore. Use Google Cloud Dataflow to query BigQuery and combine the data programmatically with the data stored in Cloud Datastore
choice_D: Store the data in a file in a regional Google Cloud Storage bucket. Use Cloud Dataflow to query BigQuery and combine the data programmatically with the data stored in Google Cloud Storage.
explanation: The correct choice is B because using a federated data source in BigQuery allows you to query data stored in Google Cloud Storage without needing to load it into BigQuery, which can be more cost-effective and efficient for frequently updated data. By storing the data in a regional Google Cloud Storage bucket, you can update the data every 30 minutes without incurring the costs associated with loading data into BigQuery repeatedly. This approach also provides flexibility in managing and updating the data externally while still allowing seamless integration with BigQuery for analysis. In contrast, options A, C, and D involve additional steps or resources, such as partitioned tables or Cloud Dataflow, which may increase complexity and costs.

question_text:
You are designing the database schema for a machine learning-based food ordering service that will predict what users want to eat. Here is some of the information you need to store:
✑ The user profile: What the user likes and doesn't like to eat
✑ The user account information: Name, address, preferred meal times
✑ The order information: When orders are made, from where, to whom
The database will be used to store all the transactional data of the product. You want to optimize the data schema. Which Google Cloud Platform product should you use?
choice_A: BigQuery
choice_B: Cloud SQL
choice_C: Cloud Bigtable
choice_D: Cloud Datastore
explanation: Cloud SQL is the correct choice for this scenario because it is a fully-managed relational database service that supports SQL databases like MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server. It is well-suited for transactional data that requires complex queries and joins, which are typical in applications involving user profiles, account information, and order details. Cloud SQL provides strong consistency and ACID transactions, which are essential for maintaining the integrity of transactional data. In contrast, BigQuery (A) is optimized for analytical queries, Cloud Bigtable (C) is designed for large-scale, low-latency workloads, and Cloud Datastore (D) is a NoSQL database that is better suited for non-relational data.

question_text: Your company is loading comma-separated values (CSV) files into Google BigQuery. The data is fully imported successfully; however, the imported data is not matching byte-to-byte to the source file. What is the most likely cause of this problem?
choice_A: The CSV data loaded in BigQuery is not flagged as CSV.
choice_B: The CSV data has invalid rows that were skipped on import.
choice_C: The CSV data loaded in BigQuery is not using BigQuery's default encoding.
choice_D: The CSV data has not gone through an ETL phase before loading into BigQuery.
explanation: The most likely cause of the imported data not matching byte-to-byte with the source file is related to encoding issues, which is addressed by choice C. BigQuery expects CSV files to be encoded in UTF-8 by default. If the source CSV file uses a different encoding, such as ISO-8859-1 or Windows-1252, the byte representation of characters may differ, leading to discrepancies when the data is loaded into BigQuery. Choices A and B are less likely causes because the CSV format and invalid rows would typically result in errors or warnings rather than subtle data mismatches. Choice D is unrelated to encoding issues, as ETL processes are not required for direct CSV imports into BigQuery.

question_text:
Your company produces 20,000 files every hour. Each data file is formatted as a comma separated values (CSV) file that is less than 4 KB. All files must be ingested on Google Cloud Platform before they can be processed. Your company site has a 200 ms latency to Google Cloud, and your Internet connection bandwidth is limited as 50 Mbps. You currently deploy a secure FTP (SFTP) server on a virtual machine in Google Compute Engine as the data ingestion point. A local SFTP client runs on a dedicated machine to transmit the CSV files as is. The goal is to make reports with data from the previous day available to the executives by 10:00 a.m. each day. This design is barely able to keep up with the current volume, even though the bandwidth utilization is rather low.
You are told that due to seasonality, your company expects the number of files to double for the next three months. Which two actions should you take? (Choose two.)
choice_A: Introduce data compression for each file to increase the rate file of file transfer.
choice_B: Contact your internet service provider (ISP) to increase your maximum bandwidth to at least 100 Mbps.
choice_C: Redesign the data ingestion process to use gsutil tool to send the CSV files to a storage bucket in parallel.
choice_D: Assemble 1,000 files into a tape archive (TAR) file. Transmit the TAR files instead, and disassemble the CSV files in the cloud upon receiving them.
choice_E: Create an S3-compatible storage endpoint in your network, and use Google Cloud Storage Transfer Service to transfer on-premises data to the designated storage bucket.
explanation: The current setup is struggling due to the sheer volume of files and the latency involved in transferring them individually. By redesigning the data ingestion process to use the `gsutil` tool (Choice C), you can leverage parallel uploads to Google Cloud Storage, which can significantly increase throughput and efficiency. Additionally, assembling 1,000 files into a TAR file (Choice D) reduces the number of individual transfers, thereby minimizing the impact of latency and overhead associated with each file transfer. This approach allows you to efficiently utilize the available bandwidth and manage the increased volume of data expected due to seasonality.

question_text:
An external customer provides you with a daily dump of data from their database. The data flows into Google Cloud Storage GCS as comma-separated values
(CSV) files. You want to analyze this data in Google BigQuery, but the data could have rows that are formatted incorrectly or corrupted. How should you build this pipeline?
choice_A: Use federated data sources, and check data in the SQL query.
choice_B: Enable BigQuery monitoring in Google Stackdriver and create an alert.
choice_C: Import the data into BigQuery using the gcloud CLI and set max_bad_records to 0.
choice_D: Run a Google Cloud Dataflow batch pipeline to import the data into BigQuery, and push errors to another dead-letter table for analysis.
explanation: The correct answer is D because using a Google Cloud Dataflow batch pipeline allows for robust data processing and transformation before loading the data into BigQuery. This approach can handle and filter out incorrectly formatted or corrupted rows, ensuring that only clean data is imported. By pushing errors to a dead-letter table, you can analyze and address data issues separately without interrupting the main data pipeline. In contrast, choice A (using federated data sources) does not provide a mechanism for handling bad records before querying, and choice C (setting max_bad_records to 0) would cause the import to fail on encountering any bad records. Choice B involves monitoring but does not address the need for data cleaning and error handling directly.

question_text:
You are choosing a NoSQL database to handle telemetry data submitted from millions of Internet-of-Things (IoT) devices. The volume of data is growing at 100
TB per year, and each data entry has about 100 attributes. The data processing pipeline does not require atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability (ACID).
However, high availability and low latency are required.
You need to analyze the data by querying against individual fields. Which three databases meet your requirements? (Choose three.)
choice_A: Redis
choice_B: HBase
choice_C: MySQL
choice_D: MongoDB
choice_E: Cassandra
explanation: The correct choices for handling telemetry data from IoT devices in this scenario are B. HBase, D. MongoDB, and E. Cassandra. HBase is a distributed, scalable, and high-performance NoSQL database that is well-suited for handling large volumes of data and provides low latency access, making it ideal for IoT applications. MongoDB is a document-oriented database that offers flexibility with its schema-less design, allowing for efficient querying of individual fields, and it supports high availability through its replica sets. Cassandra is a wide-column store that excels in handling large-scale data with high write and read throughput, providing high availability and low latency through its decentralized architecture. These databases are designed to handle large volumes of data efficiently without requiring ACID compliance, making them suitable for the given requirements.

question_text: You are training a spam classifier. You notice that you are overfitting the training data. Which three actions can you take to resolve this problem? (Choose three.)
choice_A: Get more training examples
choice_B: Reduce the number of training examples
choice_C: Use a smaller set of features
choice_D: Use a larger set of features
choice_E: Increase the regularization parameters
explanation: Overfitting occurs when a model learns the training data too well, capturing noise and details that do not generalize to new data. To address overfitting, you can get more training examples (Choice A), which helps the model learn a more general pattern rather than memorizing the training data. Using a smaller set of features (Choice C) can reduce the model's complexity, making it less likely to fit noise in the data. Increasing the regularization parameters (Choice E) adds a penalty for complexity, encouraging the model to maintain simpler, more generalizable patterns. These actions collectively help in reducing overfitting and improving the model's performance on unseen data.

question_text:
You are implementing security best practices on your data pipeline. Currently, you are manually executing jobs as the Project Owner. You want to automate these jobs by taking nightly batch files containing non-public information from Google Cloud Storage, processing them with a Spark Scala job on a Google Cloud
Dataproc cluster, and depositing the results into Google BigQuery.
How should you securely run this workload?
choice_A: Restrict the Google Cloud Storage bucket so only you can see the files
choice_B: Grant the Project Owner role to a service account, and run the job with it
choice_C: Use a service account with the ability to read the batch files and to write to BigQuery
choice_D: Use a user account with the Project Viewer role on the Cloud Dataproc cluster to read the batch files and write to BigQuery
explanation: The correct answer is C because using a service account with specific permissions is a best practice for securely automating tasks in Google Cloud. A service account can be granted the minimum necessary permissions to read the batch files from Google Cloud Storage and write the results to BigQuery, adhering to the principle of least privilege. This approach avoids using overly broad roles like Project Owner (as in B) or user accounts (as in D), which can pose security risks. Additionally, restricting access to the Google Cloud Storage bucket to only yourself (as in A) would not facilitate automation, as the service account needs access to perform the tasks.

question_text:
You are using Google BigQuery as your data warehouse. Your users report that the following simple query is running very slowly, no matter when they run the query:
SELECT country, state, city FROM [myproject:mydataset.mytable] GROUP BY country
You check the query plan for the query and see the following output in the Read section of Stage:1:
//IMG//

What is the most likely cause of the delay for this query?
choice_A: Users are running too many concurrent queries in the system
choice_B: The [myproject:mydataset.mytable] table has too many partitions
choice_C: Either the state or the city columns in the [myproject:mydataset.mytable] table have too many NULL values
choice_D: Most rows in the [myproject:mydataset.mytable] table have the same value in the country column, causing data skew
explanation: The correct answer is D. Most rows in the [myproject:mydataset.mytable] table have the same value in the country column, causing data skew. In a GROUP BY operation, BigQuery needs to redistribute data based on the grouping column, which can lead to performance issues if there is data skew. If most rows have the same value in the country column, it results in uneven distribution of data across processing nodes, causing some nodes to handle much more data than others, leading to delays. Choice A is less likely because concurrent queries would affect overall system performance, not just this specific query. Choice B is irrelevant because partitions affect query performance based on filtering, not grouping. Choice C is also unlikely because NULL values do not inherently slow down GROUP BY operations.

question_text: Your globally distributed auction application allows users to bid on items. Occasionally, users place identical bids at nearly identical times, and different application servers process those bids. Each bid event contains the item, amount, user, and timestamp. You want to collate those bid events into a single location in real time to determine which user bid first. What should you do?
choice_A: Create a file on a shared file and have the application servers write all bid events to that file. Process the file with Apache Hadoop to identify which user bid first.
choice_B: Have each application server write the bid events to Cloud Pub/Sub as they occur. Push the events from Cloud Pub/Sub to a custom endpoint that writes the bid event information into Cloud SQL.
choice_C: Set up a MySQL database for each application server to write bid events into. Periodically query each of those distributed MySQL databases and update a master MySQL database with bid event information.
choice_D: Have each application server write the bid events to Google Cloud Pub/Sub as they occur. Use a pull subscription to pull the bid events using Google Cloud Dataflow. Give the bid for each item to the user in the bid event that is processed first.
explanation: The correct answer is D because it leverages Google Cloud Pub/Sub and Google Cloud Dataflow to handle real-time bid event processing efficiently. By writing bid events to Pub/Sub, you ensure that all events are collected in a centralized, scalable messaging system. Using a pull subscription with Dataflow allows for real-time processing and analysis of these events, ensuring that the first bid processed is identified accurately. This approach is more efficient and scalable compared to A, which involves batch processing with Hadoop, and C, which requires complex synchronization between multiple MySQL databases. Option B, while similar, does not utilize Dataflow for real-time processing, which is crucial for determining the first bid accurately.

question_text: Your organization has been collecting and analyzing data in Google BigQuery for 6 months. The majority of the data analyzed is placed in a time-partitioned table named events_partitioned. To reduce the cost of queries, your organization created a view called events, which queries only the last 14 days of data. The view is described in legacy SQL. Next month, existing applications will be connecting to BigQuery to read the events data via an ODBC connection. You need to ensure the applications can connect. Which two actions should you take? (Choose two.)
choice_A: Create a new view over events using standard SQL
choice_B: Create a new partitioned table using a standard SQL query
choice_C: Create a new view over events_partitioned using standard SQL
choice_D: Create a service account for the ODBC connection to use for authentication
choice_E: Create a Google Cloud Identity and Access Management (Cloud IAM) role for the ODBC connection and shared ג€eventsג€
explanation: The correct actions to take are C and D. Action C, creating a new view over `events_partitioned` using standard SQL, is necessary because ODBC connections require standard SQL syntax, and the existing view is described in legacy SQL. This ensures compatibility with the applications connecting via ODBC. Action D, creating a service account for the ODBC connection to use for authentication, is essential for securely managing access to BigQuery. Service accounts provide a way to authenticate applications and grant them the necessary permissions to access the data. Choices A and B are not directly related to the requirements for ODBC connectivity and data access management. Choice E is incorrect because creating a Cloud IAM role alone does not address the need for a compatible SQL view or secure authentication.

question_text: You have enabled the free integration between Firebase Analytics and Google BigQuery. Firebase now automatically creates a new table daily in BigQuery in the format app_events_YYYYMMDD. You want to query all of the tables for the past 30 days in legacy SQL. What should you do?
choice_A: Use the TABLE_DATE_RANGE function
choice_B: Use the WHERE_PARTITIONTIME pseudo column
choice_C: Use WHERE date BETWEEN YYYY-MM-DD AND YYYY-MM-DD
choice_D: Use SELECT IF.(date >= YYYY-MM-DD AND date <= YYYY-MM-DD
explanation: The correct answer is A. Use the TABLE_DATE_RANGE function because, in legacy SQL, this function is specifically designed to query multiple tables that follow a date-based naming convention, such as the app_events_YYYYMMDD format used by Firebase Analytics in BigQuery. The TABLE_DATE_RANGE function allows you to specify a range of dates, and it will automatically include all tables within that range in the query. Choices B, C, and D are not applicable in this context because they either refer to standard SQL features (like WHERE_PARTITIONTIME) or do not directly address querying multiple date-named tables in legacy SQL. Therefore, TABLE_DATE_RANGE is the appropriate choice for querying tables over a specified date range in legacy SQL.

question_text: Your company is currently setting up data pipelines for their campaign. For all the Google Cloud Pub/Sub streaming data, one of the important business requirements is to be able to periodically identify the inputs and their timings during their campaign. Engineers have decided to use windowing and transformation in Google Cloud Dataflow for this purpose. However, when testing this feature, they find that the Cloud Dataflow job fails for the all streaming insert. What is the most likely cause of this problem?
choice_A: They have not assigned the timestamp, which causes the job to fail
choice_B: They have not set the triggers to accommodate the data coming in late, which causes the job to fail
choice_C: They have not applied a global windowing function, which causes the job to fail when the pipeline is created
choice_D: They have not applied a non-global windowing function, which causes the job to fail when the pipeline is created
explanation: The correct answer is D. In Google Cloud Dataflow, when processing streaming data, a non-global windowing function is typically required to organize data into logical windows for processing, especially when using transformations that rely on windowed data, such as aggregations. If a global windowing function is applied, it means that all data is treated as belonging to a single, continuous window, which can lead to inefficiencies and potential failures when the system attempts to process large volumes of streaming data without any segmentation. The failure occurs because the pipeline expects windowed data for certain operations, and without a non-global windowing function, the system cannot properly manage and process the incoming data streams. This contrasts with choice A, which involves timestamp assignment, choice B, which involves trigger settings for late data, and choice C, which incorrectly suggests that a global windowing function is needed.

question_text: You architect a system to analyze seismic data. Your extract, transform, and load (ETL) process runs as a series of MapReduce jobs on an Apache Hadoop cluster. The ETL process takes days to process a data set because some steps are computationally expensive. Then you discover that a sensor calibration step has been omitted. How should you change your ETL process to carry out sensor calibration systematically in the future?
choice_A: Modify the transformMapReduce jobs to apply sensor calibration before they do anything else.
choice_B: Introduce a new MapReduce job to apply sensor calibration to raw data, and ensure all other MapReduce jobs are chained after this.
choice_C: Add sensor calibration data to the output of the ETL process, and document that all users need to apply sensor calibration themselves.
choice_D: Develop an algorithm through simulation to predict variance of data output from the last MapReduce job based on calibration factors, and apply the correction to all data.
explanation: The correct answer is B because introducing a new MapReduce job specifically for sensor calibration ensures that calibration is systematically applied to all raw data before any further processing steps. This approach maintains the integrity of the data pipeline by ensuring that all subsequent transformations and analyses are based on calibrated data, which is crucial for accurate results. Option A, modifying existing jobs, could lead to inconsistent application of calibration and complicate the job logic. Option C, adding calibration data to the output, places the burden on users and risks inconsistent application. Option D, using a predictive algorithm, is less reliable as it applies corrections post hoc rather than ensuring data accuracy from the outset.

question_text: An online retailer has built their current application on Google App Engine. A new initiative at the company mandates that they extend their application to allow their customers to transact directly via the application. They need to manage their shopping transactions and analyze combined data from multiple datasets using a business intelligence (BI) tool. They want to use only a single database for this purpose. Which Google Cloud database should they choose?
choice_A: BigQuery
choice_B: Cloud SQL
choice_C: Cloud BigTable
choice_D: Cloud Datastore
explanation: Cloud SQL is the correct choice for the retailer's needs because it is a fully-managed relational database service that supports transactional workloads, which are essential for managing shopping transactions. It is compatible with MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server, making it suitable for applications that require complex queries and data analysis. While BigQuery (A) is excellent for large-scale data analysis, it is not optimized for transactional operations. Cloud BigTable (C) is designed for large-scale analytical and operational workloads but lacks support for complex transactions. Cloud Datastore (D) is a NoSQL database that is not ideal for transactional consistency required in shopping transactions. Therefore, Cloud SQL is the best fit for combining transactional management with the ability to analyze data using a BI tool.

question_text: Your weather app queries a database every 15 minutes to get the current temperature. The frontend is powered by Google App Engine and server millions of users. How should you design the frontend to respond to a database failure?
choice_A: Issue a command to restart the database servers.
choice_B: Retry the query with exponential backoff, up to a cap of 15 minutes.
choice_C: Retry the query every second until it comes back online to minimize staleness of data.
choice_D: Reduce the query frequency to once every hour until the database comes back online.
explanation: The correct approach is to retry the query with exponential backoff, up to a cap of 15 minutes (Choice B). This strategy helps manage load on the database by gradually increasing the time between retries, which is crucial during a failure to prevent overwhelming the system. Exponential backoff is a common pattern for handling transient failures, as it balances the need to retry with the risk of causing additional strain. Choice A, issuing a command to restart the database servers, is not feasible from the frontend and could disrupt other services. Choice C, retrying every second, could exacerbate the problem by flooding the database with requests. Choice D, reducing the query frequency to once every hour, could lead to excessively stale data, which is not ideal for a weather app that needs timely updates.

question_text: You launched a new gaming app almost three years ago. You have been uploading log files from the previous day to a separate Google BigQuery table with the table name format LOGS_yyyymmdd. You have been using table wildcard functions to generate daily and monthly reports for all time ranges. Recently, you discovered that some queries that cover long date ranges are exceeding the limit of 1,000 tables and failing. How can you resolve this issue?
choice_A: Convert all daily log tables into date-partitioned tables
choice_B: Convert the sharded tables into a single partitioned table
choice_C: Enable query caching so you can cache data from previous months
choice_D: Create separate views to cover each month, and query from these views
explanation: The correct answer is B. Convert the sharded tables into a single partitioned table. This approach addresses the issue of exceeding the 1,000 table limit by consolidating the numerous daily log tables into a single table that uses date-based partitioning. Partitioned tables in BigQuery allow for efficient querying over large date ranges without hitting the table limit, as they treat partitions as segments within a single table rather than separate tables. Option A, converting to date-partitioned tables, is similar but less precise than B, as it doesn't specify consolidating into one table. Option C, enabling query caching, does not address the table limit issue, and option D, creating separate views, would still involve querying multiple tables and not solve the problem of exceeding the table count limit.

question_text:
Your analytics team wants to build a simple statistical model to determine which customers are most likely to work with your company again, based on a few different metrics. They want to run the model on Apache Spark, using data housed in Google Cloud Storage, and you have recommended using Google Cloud
Dataproc to execute this job. Testing has shown that this workload can run in approximately 30 minutes on a 15-node cluster, outputting the results into Google
BigQuery. The plan is to run this workload weekly. How should you optimize the cluster for cost?
choice_A: Migrate the workload to Google Cloud Dataflow
choice_B: Use pre-emptible virtual machines (VMs) for the cluster
choice_C: Use a higher-memory node so that the job runs faster
choice_D: Use SSDs on the worker nodes so that the job can run faster
explanation: The correct answer is B. Use pre-emptible virtual machines (VMs) for the cluster. Pre-emptible VMs are significantly cheaper than regular VMs, which makes them an ideal choice for cost optimization, especially for workloads like this that are not time-sensitive and can tolerate interruptions. Since the job runs weekly and completes in about 30 minutes, the risk of pre-emptible VMs being terminated before completion is low, making them a cost-effective option. Options C and D, using higher-memory nodes or SSDs, would increase the cost without necessarily providing a proportional benefit in this context, as the job already completes within an acceptable timeframe. Option A, migrating to Google Cloud Dataflow, would involve additional complexity and may not necessarily reduce costs compared to using pre-emptible VMs on Dataproc.

question_text:
Your company receives both batch- and stream-based event data. You want to process the data using Google Cloud Dataflow over a predictable time period.
However, you realize that in some instances data can arrive late or out of order. How should you design your Cloud Dataflow pipeline to handle data that is late or out of order?
choice_A: Set a single global window to capture all the data.
choice_B: Set sliding windows to capture all the lagged data.
choice_C: Use watermarks and timestamps to capture the lagged data.
choice_D: Ensure every datasource type (stream or batch) has a timestamp, and use the timestamps to define the logic for lagged data.
explanation: The correct answer is C. Use watermarks and timestamps to capture the lagged data. In Google Cloud Dataflow, watermarks are used to track the progress of event time in the pipeline, allowing the system to estimate the completeness of data. By using watermarks in conjunction with timestamps, you can effectively manage and process late-arriving data by defining how long to wait for late data before closing a window. This approach is more precise than setting a single global window (Choice A), which would not handle late data efficiently, or using sliding windows (Choice B), which may not adequately address out-of-order events. While ensuring every data source has a timestamp (Choice D) is important, it does not inherently manage late or out-of-order data without the use of watermarks.

question_text:
You have some data, which is shown in the graphic below. The two dimensions are X and Y, and the shade of each dot represents what class it is. You want to classify this data accurately using a linear algorithm. To do this you need to add a synthetic feature. What should the value of that feature be?
//IMG//
choice_A: X2+Y2
choice_B: X2
choice_C: Y2
choice_D: cos(X)
explanation: In the given scenario, the data points are likely not linearly separable in the original two-dimensional space defined by X and Y. To achieve linear separability, a synthetic feature that transforms the data into a higher-dimensional space is needed. Among the given choices, A. X² + Y² is the correct synthetic feature because it represents the equation of a circle in the X-Y plane. This transformation can help separate classes that are distributed in a circular pattern, allowing a linear algorithm to classify the data accurately. The other options, B. X², C. Y², and D. cos(X), do not provide the necessary transformation to achieve linear separability for circularly distributed data.

question_text: You are integrating one of your internal IT applications and Google BigQuery, so users can query BigQuery from the application's interface. You do not want individual users to authenticate to BigQuery and you do not want to give them access to the dataset. You need to securely access BigQuery from your IT application. What should you do?
choice_A: Create groups for your users and give those groups access to the dataset
choice_B: Integrate with a single sign-on (SSO) platform, and pass each user's credentials along with the query request
choice_C: Create a service account and grant dataset access to that account. Use the service account's private key to access the dataset
choice_D: Create a dummy user and grant dataset access to that user. Store the username and password for that user in a file on the files system, and use those credentials to access the BigQuery dataset
explanation: The correct approach to securely access Google BigQuery from an internal IT application without requiring individual user authentication is to use a service account, as described in choice C. A service account is a special Google account that belongs to your application or a virtual machine (VM), instead of to an individual end user. By granting dataset access to the service account and using its private key, you ensure that the application can authenticate and access BigQuery securely without exposing individual user credentials or requiring users to have direct access to the dataset. This method is more secure and manageable compared to storing credentials in a file (choice D) or managing user groups (choice A). Integrating with SSO (choice B) would still involve individual user authentication, which is not desired in this scenario.

question_text: You are building a data pipeline on Google Cloud. You need to prepare data using a casual method for a machine-learning process. You want to support a logistic regression model. You also need to monitor and adjust for null values, which must remain real-valued and cannot be removed. What should you do?
choice_A: Use Cloud Dataprep to find null values in sample source data. Convert all nulls to 'none' using a Cloud Dataproc job.
choice_B: Use Cloud Dataprep to find null values in sample source data. Convert all nulls to 0 using a Cloud Dataprep job.
choice_C: Use Cloud Dataflow to find null values in sample source data. Convert all nulls to 'none' using a Cloud Dataprep job.
choice_D: Use Cloud Dataflow to find null values in sample source data. Convert all nulls to 0 using a custom script.
explanation: The correct answer is B because Cloud Dataprep is a tool designed for visually exploring, cleaning, and preparing data, making it suitable for identifying and handling null values in your dataset. Converting null values to 0 is appropriate for a logistic regression model, as it requires real-valued inputs and cannot handle nulls directly. By using Cloud Dataprep to convert nulls to 0, you maintain the real-valued nature of the data, which is essential for the model's performance. Options A and C suggest converting nulls to 'none', which is not suitable for logistic regression as it requires numeric inputs. Option D involves using a custom script in Cloud Dataflow, which is more complex and unnecessary when Cloud Dataprep can handle the task efficiently.

question_text: You set up a streaming data insert into a Redis cluster via a Kafka cluster. Both clusters are running on Compute Engine instances. You need to encrypt data at rest with encryption keys that you can create, rotate, and destroy as needed. What should you do?
choice_A: Create a dedicated service account, and use encryption at rest to reference your data stored in your Compute Engine cluster instances as part of your API service calls.
choice_B: Create encryption keys in Cloud Key Management Service. Use those keys to encrypt your data in all of the Compute Engine cluster instances.
choice_C: Create encryption keys locally. Upload your encryption keys to Cloud Key Management Service. Use those keys to encrypt your data in all of the Compute Engine cluster instances.
choice_D: Create encryption keys in Cloud Key Management Service. Reference those keys in your API service calls when accessing the data in your Compute Engine cluster instances.
explanation: The correct answer is B. Creating encryption keys in Cloud Key Management Service (KMS) and using them to encrypt data in Compute Engine cluster instances ensures that the data at rest is securely encrypted with keys that can be managed (created, rotated, and destroyed) as needed. Cloud KMS is a managed service that allows you to centrally manage and control your encryption keys, providing a secure and scalable solution for encryption needs. Option A is incorrect because it does not specify the use of a key management service for encryption. Option C is not optimal because managing encryption keys locally can be less secure and more cumbersome than using a managed service like Cloud KMS. Option D incorrectly suggests referencing keys in API calls, which is not a standard practice for encrypting data at rest.

question_text: You are developing an application that uses a recommendation engine on Google Cloud. Your solution should display new videos to customers based on past views. Your solution needs to generate labels for the entities in videos that the customer has viewed. Your design must be able to provide very fast filtering suggestions based on data from other customer preferences on several TB of data. What should you do?
choice_A: Build and train a complex classification model with Spark MLlib to generate labels and filter the results. Deploy the models using Cloud Dataproc. Call the model from your application.
choice_B: Build and train a classification model with Spark MLlib to generate labels. Build and train a second classification model with Spark MLlib to filter results to match customer preferences. Deploy the models using Cloud Dataproc. Call the models from your application.
choice_C: Build an application that calls the Cloud Video Intelligence API to generate labels. Store data in Cloud Bigtable, and filter the predicted labels to match the user's viewing history to generate preferences.
choice_D: Build an application that calls the Cloud Video Intelligence API to generate labels. Store data in Cloud SQL, and join and filter the predicted labels to match the user's viewing history to generate preferences.
explanation: The correct answer is C because it leverages the Cloud Video Intelligence API to efficiently generate labels for video content, which is a suitable approach for handling large-scale video data. Cloud Bigtable is an ideal choice for storing and quickly accessing large volumes of structured data, making it well-suited for filtering and retrieving user preferences based on past viewing history. This combination allows for fast filtering and recommendation generation, which is essential for applications dealing with several terabytes of data. In contrast, options A and B involve building and deploying custom models with Spark MLlib, which may not be as efficient or scalable for this specific use case. Option D, while using the Cloud Video Intelligence API, suggests using Cloud SQL, which may not perform as well as Cloud Bigtable for handling large-scale data filtering and retrieval tasks.

question_text: You are selecting services to write and transform JSON messages from Cloud Pub/Sub to BigQuery for a data pipeline on Google Cloud. You want to minimize service costs. You also want to monitor and accommodate input data volume that will vary in size with minimal manual intervention. What should you do?
choice_A: Use Cloud Dataproc to run your transformations. Monitor CPU utilization for the cluster. Resize the number of worker nodes in your cluster via the command line.
choice_B: Use Cloud Dataproc to run your transformations. Use the diagnose command to generate an operational output archive. Locate the bottleneck and adjust cluster resources.
choice_C: Use Cloud Dataflow to run your transformations. Monitor the job system lag with Stackdriver. Use the default autoscaling setting for worker instances.
choice_D: Use Cloud Dataflow to run your transformations. Monitor the total execution time for a sampling of jobs. Configure the job to use non-default Compute Engine machine types when needed.
explanation: The correct choice is C because Cloud Dataflow is a fully managed service that supports autoscaling, which helps minimize costs by automatically adjusting the number of worker instances based on the current workload. This feature is particularly useful for handling varying input data volumes with minimal manual intervention. Monitoring job system lag with Stackdriver (now known as Google Cloud Monitoring) allows you to track the performance and efficiency of your data pipeline, ensuring that it accommodates changes in data volume effectively. In contrast, options A and B involve manual intervention to resize clusters in Cloud Dataproc, which can be less cost-effective and more labor-intensive. Option D suggests using non-default machine types, which may increase costs unnecessarily without addressing the need for automatic scaling.

question_text: Your infrastructure includes a set of YouTube channels. You have been tasked with creating a process for sending the YouTube channel data to Google Cloud for analysis. You want to design a solution that allows your world-wide marketing teams to perform ANSI SQL and other types of analysis on up-to-date YouTube channels log data. How should you set up the log data transfer into Google Cloud?
choice_A: Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi-Regional storage bucket as a final destination.
choice_B: Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Regional bucket as a final destination.
choice_C: Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi-Regional storage bucket as a final destination.
choice_D: Use BigQuery Data Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Regional storage bucket as a final destination.
explanation: The correct answer is A. Use Storage Transfer Service to transfer the offsite backup files to a Cloud Storage Multi-Regional storage bucket as a final destination. This choice is appropriate because the Storage Transfer Service is designed to efficiently transfer large amounts of data from external sources to Google Cloud Storage. By using a Multi-Regional storage bucket, you ensure that the data is highly available and can be accessed quickly from anywhere in the world, which is essential for global marketing teams. Choices C and D incorrectly suggest using BigQuery Data Transfer Service, which is not designed for transferring files from external sources to Cloud Storage. Choice B, while using Storage Transfer Service, suggests a Regional bucket, which may not provide the same level of availability and access speed as a Multi-Regional bucket.

question_text: You are creating a model to predict housing prices. Due to budget constraints, you must run it on a single resource-constrained virtual machine. Which learning algorithm should you use?
choice_A: Linear regression
choice_B: Logistic classification
choice_C: Recurrent neural network
choice_D: Feedforward neural network
explanation: Linear regression is the most suitable choice for predicting housing prices on a resource-constrained virtual machine due to its simplicity and efficiency. It is a straightforward algorithm that models the relationship between input features and a continuous target variable, making it computationally less intensive compared to more complex models. In contrast, logistic classification (B) is used for binary outcomes, not continuous predictions like housing prices. Recurrent neural networks (C) and feedforward neural networks (D) are more complex and computationally demanding, which may not be feasible given the resource constraints. Therefore, linear regression (A) is the best option for this scenario.

question_text: You are designing storage for very large text files for a data pipeline on Google Cloud. You want to support ANSI SQL queries. You also want to support compression and parallel load from the input locations using Google recommended practices. What should you do?
choice_A: Transform text files to compressed Avro using Cloud Dataflow. Use BigQuery for storage and query.
choice_B: Transform text files to compressed Avro using Cloud Dataflow. Use Cloud Storage and BigQuery permanent linked tables for query.
choice_C: Compress text files to gzip using the Grid Computing Tools. Use BigQuery for storage and query.
choice_D: Compress text files to gzip using the Grid Computing Tools. Use Cloud Storage, and then import into Cloud Bigtable for query.
explanation: The correct answer is B because it aligns with Google Cloud's recommended practices for handling large datasets while supporting ANSI SQL queries. Transforming text files to compressed Avro format using Cloud Dataflow optimizes the data for efficient storage and querying. Storing the data in Cloud Storage and using BigQuery permanent linked tables allows for seamless integration and querying capabilities without the need to load the data into BigQuery, thus supporting parallel loading and efficient querying. This approach leverages the strengths of both Cloud Storage for cost-effective storage and BigQuery for powerful SQL querying. In contrast, options A and C involve storing data directly in BigQuery, which may not be as cost-effective for very large datasets, and option D suggests using Cloud Bigtable, which is not optimized for ANSI SQL queries.

question_text: You are developing an application on Google Cloud that will automatically generate subject labels for users' blog posts. You are under competitive pressure to add this feature quickly, and you have no additional developer resources. No one on your team has experience with machine learning. What should you do?
choice_A: Call the Cloud Natural Language API from your application. Process the generated Entity Analysis as labels.
choice_B: Call the Cloud Natural Language API from your application. Process the generated Sentiment Analysis as labels.
choice_C: Build and train a text classification model using TensorFlow. Deploy the model using Cloud Machine Learning Engine. Call the model from your application and process the results as labels.
choice_D: Build and train a text classification model using TensorFlow. Deploy the model using a Kubernetes Engine cluster. Call the model from your application and process the results as labels.
explanation: The correct choice is A because the Cloud Natural Language API provides a pre-trained model that can analyze text and extract entities, which can be used as subject labels for blog posts. This approach is ideal given the constraints of needing a quick implementation and lacking machine learning expertise on the team. Option B, using Sentiment Analysis, is not suitable for generating subject labels as it focuses on determining the sentiment rather than identifying key topics or entities. Options C and D involve building and training custom models with TensorFlow, which requires significant time, expertise, and resources that the team does not have. Therefore, leveraging the existing capabilities of the Cloud Natural Language API is the most efficient and effective solution.

question_text: You are designing storage for 20 TB of text files as part of deploying a data pipeline on Google Cloud. Your input data is in CSV format. You want to minimize the cost of querying aggregate values for multiple users who will query the data in Cloud Storage with multiple engines. Which storage service and schema design should you use?
choice_A: Use Cloud Bigtable for storage. Install the HBase shell on a Compute Engine instance to query the Cloud Bigtable data.
choice_B: Use Cloud Bigtable for storage. Link as permanent tables in BigQuery for query.
choice_C: Use Cloud Storage for storage. Link as permanent tables in BigQuery for query.
choice_D: Use Cloud Storage for storage. Link as temporary tables in BigQuery for query.
explanation: The correct choice is C because using Cloud Storage for storing the CSV files is cost-effective for large datasets like 20 TB. By linking these files as permanent tables in BigQuery, you enable efficient querying capabilities without needing to move or duplicate the data, which minimizes costs associated with data transfer and storage. Permanent tables in BigQuery allow for optimized query performance and caching, which is beneficial for multiple users querying aggregate values. In contrast, Cloud Bigtable (options A and B) is more suited for low-latency, high-throughput workloads rather than analytical queries, and using temporary tables (option D) would incur repeated costs for each query session.

question_text:
You are designing storage for two relational tables that are part of a 10-TB database on Google Cloud. You want to support transactions that scale horizontally.
You also want to optimize data for range queries on non-key columns. What should you do?
choice_A: Use Cloud SQL for storage. Add secondary indexes to support query patterns.
choice_B: Use Cloud SQL for storage. Use Cloud Dataflow to transform data to support query patterns.
choice_C: Use Cloud Spanner for storage. Add secondary indexes to support query patterns.
choice_D: Use Cloud Spanner for storage. Use Cloud Dataflow to transform data to support query patterns.
explanation: The correct answer is C. Use Cloud Spanner for storage. Add secondary indexes to support query patterns. Cloud Spanner is a horizontally scalable, globally distributed, and strongly consistent database service that supports transactions, making it suitable for large-scale databases like a 10-TB database. By adding secondary indexes, you can optimize data access for range queries on non-key columns, improving query performance without restructuring the data. Cloud SQL, on the other hand, is a managed relational database service that may not scale as efficiently for very large datasets and high transaction volumes compared to Cloud Spanner. Using Cloud Dataflow to transform data (as suggested in options B and D) is unnecessary for optimizing query patterns when secondary indexes can directly address the need.

question_text:
Your financial services company is moving to cloud technology and wants to store 50 TB of financial time-series data in the cloud. This data is updated frequently and new data will be streaming in all the time. Your company also wants to move their existing Apache Hadoop jobs to the cloud to get insights into this data.
Which product should they use to store the data?
choice_A: Cloud Bigtable
choice_B: Google BigQuery
choice_C: Google Cloud Storage
choice_D: Google Cloud Datastore
explanation: Cloud Bigtable is the correct choice for storing 50 TB of financial time-series data in the cloud because it is designed to handle large volumes of structured data with high throughput and low latency, making it ideal for time-series data and real-time analytics. It supports seamless integration with Apache Hadoop, allowing the company to move their existing Hadoop jobs to the cloud efficiently. In contrast, Google BigQuery is optimized for analytical queries rather than frequent updates, Google Cloud Storage is more suited for unstructured data storage, and Google Cloud Datastore is tailored for transactional and application data rather than large-scale analytical workloads.

question_text:
An organization maintains a Google BigQuery dataset that contains tables with user-level data. They want to expose aggregates of this data to other Google
Cloud projects, while still controlling access to the user-level data. Additionally, they need to minimize their overall storage cost and ensure the analysis cost for other projects is assigned to those projects. What should they do?
choice_A: Create and share an authorized view that provides the aggregate results.
choice_B: Create and share a new dataset and view that provides the aggregate results.
choice_C: Create and share a new dataset and table that contains the aggregate results.
choice_D: Create dataViewer Identity and Access Management (IAM) roles on the dataset to enable sharing.
explanation: The correct answer is A. Create and share an authorized view that provides the aggregate results. Authorized views in Google BigQuery allow you to share query results with specific users or projects without granting them access to the underlying tables containing sensitive user-level data. This approach ensures that only the aggregate data is exposed, maintaining the privacy of the detailed data. Additionally, using authorized views helps control storage costs, as it does not require duplicating data into new tables or datasets, unlike option C. Furthermore, by sharing the view, the analysis costs incurred by other projects are billed to those projects, aligning with the organization's requirement to assign analysis costs appropriately. Option D does not address the need to expose only aggregate data, and option B involves creating additional datasets, which could increase storage costs unnecessarily.

question_text: Government regulations in your industry mandate that you have to maintain an auditable record of access to certain types of data. Assuming that all expiring logs will be archived correctly, where should you store data that is subject to that mandate?
choice_A: Encrypted on Cloud Storage with user-supplied encryption keys. A separate decryption key will be given to each authorized user.
choice_B: In a BigQuery dataset that is viewable only by authorized personnel, with the Data Access log used to provide the auditability.
choice_C: In Cloud SQL, with separate database user names to each user. The Cloud SQL Admin activity logs will be used to provide the auditability.
choice_D: In a bucket on Cloud Storage that is accessible only by an AppEngine service that collects user information and logs the access before providing a link to the bucket.
explanation: The correct answer is B because BigQuery provides robust access control and logging capabilities that are essential for maintaining an auditable record of data access. By storing data in a BigQuery dataset that is viewable only by authorized personnel, you can leverage BigQuery's Data Access logs to track and audit who accessed the data and when. This setup ensures compliance with government regulations requiring auditable records. In contrast, options A, C, and D either do not provide the same level of built-in auditability or rely on external mechanisms that may not be as reliable or comprehensive as BigQuery's native logging features.

question_text: Your neural network model is taking days to train. You want to increase the training speed. What can you do?
choice_A: Subsample your test dataset.
choice_B: Subsample your training dataset.
choice_C: Increase the number of input features to your model.
choice_D: Increase the number of layers in your neural network.
explanation: Subsampling your training dataset (Choice B) reduces the amount of data the model needs to process, which directly decreases the training time. While this approach can speed up training, it may also lead to a less accurate model due to reduced data for learning. On the other hand, subsampling the test dataset (Choice A) does not affect training speed since the test set is not used during training. Increasing the number of input features (Choice C) or the number of layers in the neural network (Choice D) typically increases the model's complexity and computational requirements, leading to longer training times.

question_text: You are responsible for writing your company's ETL pipelines to run on an Apache Hadoop cluster. The pipeline will require some checkpointing and splitting pipelines. Which method should you use to write the pipelines?
choice_A: PigLatin using Pig
choice_B: HiveQL using Hive
choice_C: Java using MapReduce
choice_D: Python using MapReduce
explanation: PigLatin using Pig (Choice A) is the correct method for writing ETL pipelines on an Apache Hadoop cluster when checkpointing and splitting pipelines are required. PigLatin is a high-level scripting language designed specifically for processing and analyzing large datasets on Hadoop. It provides built-in support for complex data transformations, including checkpointing and splitting, which simplifies the development of ETL processes compared to the lower-level abstractions of MapReduce. While HiveQL using Hive (Choice B) is suitable for querying and managing large datasets with a SQL-like interface, it is less flexible for complex ETL tasks. Java using MapReduce (Choice C) and Python using MapReduce (Choice D) require more boilerplate code and manual handling of data flow, making them less efficient for this specific use case.

question_text:
Your company maintains a hybrid deployment with GCP, where analytics are performed on your anonymized customer data. The data are imported to Cloud
Storage from your data center through parallel uploads to a data transfer server running on GCP. Management informs you that the daily transfers take too long and have asked you to fix the problem. You want to maximize transfer speeds. Which action should you take?
choice_A: Increase the CPU size on your server.
choice_B: Increase the size of the Google Persistent Disk on your server.
choice_C: Increase your network bandwidth from your datacenter to GCP.
choice_D: Increase your network bandwidth from Compute Engine to Cloud Storage.
explanation: The correct answer is C. Increase your network bandwidth from your datacenter to GCP. The primary bottleneck in the scenario described is the speed at which data is transferred from your on-premises data center to Google Cloud Platform (GCP). By increasing the network bandwidth between your data center and GCP, you can significantly reduce the time it takes to upload data to Cloud Storage. Options A and B, which involve increasing CPU size and disk size, respectively, do not directly address the network transfer speed issue. Option D, increasing bandwidth from Compute Engine to Cloud Storage, is irrelevant in this context because the bottleneck is between the data center and GCP, not within GCP itself.

question_text: You are building new real-time data warehouse for your company and will use Google BigQuery streaming inserts. There is no guarantee that data will only be sent in once but you do have a unique ID for each row of data and an event timestamp. You want to ensure that duplicates are not included while interactively querying data. Which query type should you use?
choice_A: Include ORDER BY DESK on timestamp column and LIMIT to 1.
choice_B: Use GROUP BY on the unique ID column and timestamp column and SUM on the values.
choice_C: Use the LAG window function with PARTITION by unique ID along with WHERE LAG IS NOT NULL.
choice_D: Use the ROW_NUMBER window function with PARTITION by unique ID along with WHERE row equals 1.
explanation: The correct choice is D because using the `ROW_NUMBER` window function allows you to assign a unique sequential integer to rows within a partition of a result set, which in this case is partitioned by the unique ID. By doing so, you can effectively identify and filter out duplicate rows by selecting only those where the row number equals 1. This ensures that only the first occurrence of each unique ID is included in the query results. Option A, using `ORDER BY` and `LIMIT`, does not guarantee uniqueness across the dataset. Option B, using `GROUP BY`, would aggregate data rather than eliminate duplicates based on a unique ID. Option C, using the `LAG` function, is typically used to access data from previous rows and is not suitable for directly eliminating duplicates.

question_text:
MJTelco Case Study -

Company Overview -
MJTelco is a startup that plans to build networks in rapidly growing, underserved markets around the world. The company has patents for innovative optical communications hardware. Based on these patents, they can create many reliable, high-speed backbone links with inexpensive hardware.

Company Background -
Founded by experienced telecom executives, MJTelco uses technologies originally developed to overcome communications challenges in space. Fundamental to their operation, they need to create a distributed data infrastructure that drives real-time analysis and incorporates machine learning to continuously optimize their topologies. Because their hardware is inexpensive, they plan to overdeploy the network allowing them to account for the impact of dynamic regional politics on location availability and cost.
Their management and operations teams are situated all around the globe creating many-to-many relationship between data consumers and provides in their system. After careful consideration, they decided public cloud is the perfect environment to support their needs.

Solution Concept -
MJTelco is running a successful proof-of-concept (PoC) project in its labs. They have two primary needs:
✑ Scale and harden their PoC to support significantly more data flows generated when they ramp to more than 50,000 installations.
✑ Refine their machine-learning cycles to verify and improve the dynamic models they use to control topology definition.
MJTelco will also use three separate operating environments `" development/test, staging, and production `" to meet the needs of running experiments, deploying new features, and serving production customers.

Business Requirements -
✑ Scale up their production environment with minimal cost, instantiating resources when and where needed in an unpredictable, distributed telecom user community.
✑ Ensure security of their proprietary data to protect their leading-edge machine learning and analysis.

MJTelco is building a custom interface to share data. They have these requirements:
1. They need to do aggregations over their petabyte-scale datasets.
2. They need to scan specific time range rows with a very fast response time (milliseconds).
Which combination of Google Cloud Platform products should you recommend?
choice_A: Cloud Datastore and Cloud Bigtable
choice_B: Cloud Bigtable and Cloud SQL
choice_C: BigQuery and Cloud Bigtable
choice_D: BigQuery and Cloud Storage
explanation: The correct answer is C. BigQuery and Cloud Bigtable because these services are well-suited for handling MJTelco's requirements for large-scale data processing and fast access. BigQuery is designed for performing aggregations over petabyte-scale datasets, making it ideal for MJTelco's need to analyze vast amounts of data efficiently. Cloud Bigtable, on the other hand, is optimized for low-latency access to large datasets, allowing for fast response times when scanning specific time range rows, which aligns with MJTelco's need for millisecond-level response times. In contrast, Cloud Datastore and Cloud SQL (choices A and B) are not optimized for handling petabyte-scale data or providing the required low-latency access, and Cloud Storage (choice D) is more suited for unstructured data storage rather than fast querying and analysis.

Company Overview -
MJTelco is a startup that plans to build networks in rapidly growing, underserved markets around the world. The company has patents for innovative optical communications hardware. Based on these patents, they can create many reliable, high-speed backbone links with inexpensive hardware.

Company Background -

✑ Scale and harden their PoC to support significantly more data flows generated when they ramp to more than 50,000 installations.
Refine their machine-learning cycles to verify and improve the dynamic models they use to control topology definition.

You need to compose visualization for operations teams with the following requirements:
✑ Telemetry must include data from all 50,000 installations for the most recent 6 weeks (sampling once every minute)
✑ The report must not be more than 3 hours delayed from live data.
✑ The actionable report should only show suboptimal links.
✑ Most suboptimal links should be sorted to the top.
Suboptimal links can be grouped and filtered by regional geography.
//IMG//

✑ User response time to load the report must be <5 seconds.
You create a data source to store the last 6 weeks of data, and create visualizations that allow viewers to see multiple date ranges, distinct geographic regions, and unique installation types. You always show the latest data without any changes to your visualizations. You want to avoid creating and updating new visualizations each month. What should you do?
choice_A: Look through the current data and compose a series of charts and tables, one for each possible combination of criteria.
choice_B: Look through the current data and compose a small set of generalized charts and tables bound to criteria filters that allow value selection.
choice_C: Export the data to a spreadsheet, compose a series of charts and tables, one for each possible combination of criteria, and spread them across multiple tabs.
choice_D: Load the data into relational database tables, write a Google App Engine application that queries all rows, summarizes the data across each criteria, and then renders results using the Google Charts and visualization API.
explanation: The correct answer is B because it provides a scalable and efficient approach to visualizing data without the need for constant updates or creating numerous charts for every possible scenario. By using generalized charts and tables bound to criteria filters, users can dynamically select and view different combinations of data, such as date ranges, geographic regions, and installation types, without altering the visualizations each month. This method ensures that the latest data is always displayed and meets the requirement of a user response time of less than 5 seconds. In contrast, option A would require a cumbersome number of static charts, option C involves manual updates and lacks automation, and option D is overly complex for the task, potentially impacting performance and response time.

question_text:
MJTelco Case Study -

Company Overview -
MJTelco is a startup that plans to build networks in rapidly growing, underserved markets around the world. The company has patents for innovative optical communications hardware. Based on these patents, they can create many reliable, high-speed backbone links with inexpensive hardware.
